components.label: Mobile/Web Client
components.description: Maintains persistent WebSocket connection to chat server

components.label: API Gateway / WebSocket Manager
components.description: Manages long-lived WebSocket connections; routes to correct chat server shard

components.label: Load Balancer
components.description: Distributes WebSocket connection load across chat server fleet

components.label: Chat Server
components.description: Stateful server holding active WebSocket connections; forwards messages to queue

components.label: Message Queue (Kafka)
components.description: Pub/sub backbone — decouples sender from recipient delivery; enables fan-out

components.label: Message Store (Cassandra)
components.description: Write-optimised wide-column store for message history; partitioned by chat_id

components.label: Presence & Session Cache (Redis)
components.description: Stores online/offline status, active server mapping, undelivered message pointers

components.label: Object Storage (S3)
components.description: Stores media attachments — images, video, voice notes, documents

components.label: CDN
components.description: Delivers media from edge nodes; reduces latency for image/video downloads globally

components.label: Monitoring
components.description: Tracks message delivery rate, queue depth, WebSocket connection counts, p99 latency

keyInsights: Chat servers are stateful — each holds active WebSocket connections; the presence cache maps user→server
Cassandra's wide-column model is ideal: partition by chat_id, cluster by timestamp for efficient range reads
Offline delivery: message queue persists undelivered messages until recipient reconnects and polls
Media upload goes client→S3 directly (pre-signed URL); message payload carries only the S3 object key

tradeoffs.con: Operational complexity; adds latency vs direct server-to-server routing

tradeoffs.pro: Writes at massive scale, time-series friendly
tradeoffs.con: No joins; complex consistency model; eventual consistency requires careful conflict handling

tradeoffs.pro: No round-trip to presence store for every message
tradeoffs.con: Server failure drops all connected sessions; requires graceful reconnect logic on client

antiPatterns: Storing media blobs inside the message database — explodes storage costs and query performance
Polling the database for new messages instead of using push/WebSocket — cannot scale past 10k users
Single monolithic server for all chat connections — single point of failure at 2B users
Delivering messages synchronously in the HTTP request cycle — blocks sender until recipient confirms
